name: Event-Yield Insurance and Admission Criteria
consequences: Tightening pre-admission screening to exclude low-probability participants (e.g., requiring ≥1 event per week self-report rather than ≥2 per month, or mandatory baseline actigraphy confirmation) increases the likelihood that each bed-night yields a captured event, but risks excluding genuinely affected individuals whose episodes cluster unpredictably and may shrink the recruitment pool. Conversely, loosening criteria to capture broader phenotypes inflates unproductive admissions and consumes budget for participants who generate no events, directly eroding the €80/night compensation pool and year-two operational capacity.
options: Require all candidates to undergo 1-week outpatient actigraphy and a second clinical interview 7–14 days later to confirm event clustering, admitting only those with confirmed weekly or biweekly episodes and coherent collateral history, thereby raising event-yield probability to ≥85% but narrowing recruitment to the most reliably active participants.

Maintain current ≥2-episode/month criterion but offer extended admissions (up to 10 weeks) as the primary retention pathway rather than as a fallback, effectively converting low-yield admissions into longitudinal exposure opportunities and accepting the budget trade-off of longer stays for fewer total unique participants.

Stratify admission into two concurrent arms—a high-yield fast-track for ≥1 event per week (8-week maximum stay) and an exploratory arm for ≥2 per month (permitting 10-week stays)—allowing the fast-track to generate publications and methods validation rapidly while the exploratory arm builds phenotypic breadth, with separate success metrics for each.
review: The core tension is yield reliability versus sample representativeness: tighter screening boosts event capture per bed-night but risks missing the substantial population of individuals with sporadic or clustered episodes whose pathophysiology may differ from high-frequency cases. None of the options address what happens if the regional referral network cannot sustain 30–35 admissions per year at the chosen threshold—recruitment shortfall is treated as a binary trigger at month 18, but intermediate adjustments to the admission strategy are not designed in advance.

name: Technician Staffing Model and Night-Shift Sustainability
consequences: The current plan specifies 3 technicians rotating night shifts to cover 8 active suites with response and annotation margin. A true 3-person rotation (each on duty one-third of nights) leaves no buffer for illness, training, or personnel turnover; a 4- or 5-person rotation adds resilience but increases personnel costs (the largest budget component, ~€2M/3 years) and may exceed year-one hiring capacity. Undersized rosters risk annotation backlog, delayed event escalation, and burnout; oversized rosters consume contingency funds and leave the program vulnerable to attrition if initial grant outcomes disappoint.
options: Maintain a 3-person core with a contractual on-call backup technician available for same-day substitution and occasional extra nights, funded through contingency and recruitment bonuses, with explicit cross-training on sensor troubleshooting and annotation workflows to compensate for thin coverage.

Hire 4 full-time technicians from the start, staggering their start dates by 3–4 months to absorb training and ramp-up costs across year one, and structure overtime as discretionary (only paid during high-admission periods), reducing base salary impact while maintaining resilience.

Implement a hybrid model using 2.5 FTE resident technicians (one full-time night lead, two 0.75-time night technicians) paired with a 0.5 FTE daytime annotation specialist who scores events and manages the backlog 9 AM–5 PM on a rolling basis, allowing technicians to focus on live monitoring rather than real-time annotation.
review: The tension is cost efficiency versus operational safety: understaffing creates single-point failure risk during illness or unexpected event clustering, while overstaffing rapidly exhausts the €2M personnel budget and leaves no flexibility for unforeseen technical demands (e.g., additional sensor or network infrastructure). The options do not address how quickly the program can hire and onboard sleep technicians in Bonn's labor market, or whether mid-contract staffing adjustments (e.g., reduction from 4 to 3 technicians if recruitment stalls) trigger union or contractual complications under German employment law.

name: Video Surveillance Scope and Privacy Trade-offs
consequences: The current plan restricts bedroom video to real-time technician use and does not deposit it in public repositories due to privacy constraints, but does not specify whether video is retained after initial annotation, whether it is accessible for secondary method-development research (e.g., training computer-vision models for event detection), or how consent is framed (broad consent for video use in research versus explicit, narrow consent). Expanded video retention and research use unlocks potential for algorithmic event detection and phenotypic analysis but amplifies privacy liability and may deter participants, particularly those with partners or family members in the bedroom. Strict minimization (delete video after annotation, no secondary use) simplifies ethics compliance and protects participant privacy but forecloses opportunities for video-based algorithm development.
options: Retain all video data in encrypted on-site storage accessible only to the core team for real-time annotation and quality assurance, delete recordings 6 months post-admission, and explicitly prohibit secondary research use or export, prioritizing participant privacy and ethical minimalism over future algorithmic opportunities.

Implement a tiered consent model: standard participants consent to real-time video annotation and quality assurance with deletion after 6 months; a smaller sub-cohort (10–15 participants, separately consented) permits video retention and use by the computational team for algorithm development, with strict institutional governance and no external sharing.

Deploy video capture only in common areas (corridor, observation room) and non-private spaces during the first 8 weeks, and only in participant bedrooms during escalation nights after technician-flagged events, with explicit consent for event-specific video and automatic deletion of baseline bedroom footage after annotation, minimizing privacy exposure while preserving clinical context.
review: The core tension is research utility versus participant rights: video data dramatically improves event detection and phenotyping capacity, but video of sleep behavior in a private bedroom is inherently sensitive and difficult to de-identify. The options assume that participants can meaningfully distinguish between tiered consent categories in advance, but lived experience of privacy expectations often diverges from consent form language—none of the options include participant feedback loops or post-admission consent adjustments if privacy discomfort emerges.

name: Sensor Acquisition Tier Scheduling and Data Burden
consequences: The current plan uses nightly low-burden monitoring (dry-EEG headbands, mattress sensors, infrared cameras, microphones, environmental sensors) plus scheduled enhanced nights with full PSG on arrival, mid-stay, and discharge. Expanding enhanced-night frequency (e.g., every third night for all participants) increases physiological richness and event characterization but multiplies PSG setup time, technician workload, and equipment wear; scaling down scheduled PSG while relying on escalation nights only (reactive full PSG after technician-flagged events) reduces routine burden but risks missing multi-event nights and baseline arousal metrics if events are infrequent or missed by the technician. Participant comfort also trades off: full PSG montages (scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, leg EMG) are more burdensome than dry headbands and may increase dropout or movement artifact.
options: Perform full PSG montage on all participants on arrival night and final night only, with one mid-stay enhanced night per 2-week block, and escalate to full PSG within 24 hours of any technician-flagged event; prioritize data quality over schedule standardization and accept variability in PSG frequency across participants.

Implement a stratified enhancement protocol: high-event-yield participants (≥1 event/week based on early admission data) receive full PSG every fourth night; exploratory-arm participants receive full PSG on arrival, mid-stay, and discharge only; all participants receive escalation PSG after flagged events; this maximizes data richness for high-yield cases while conserving technician time for lower-yield admissions.

Introduce a 2-night baseline PSG (arrival and night 2) to capture sleep-architecture stability, then shift to nightly low-burden monitoring with reactive full PSG only upon technician-flagged events, reducing routine setup burden and equipment cycles while assuming that inter-rater scoring of low-burden signals is sufficiently reliable to characterize baseline patterns.
review: The tension is scientific comprehensiveness versus operational scalability: PSG is the gold standard for physiology but is time-intensive and participant-burdensome, while low-burden sensors are more naturalistic but lack the physiological specificity to confidently rule out mimics (e.g., focal seizures with behavioral features). None of the options quantify the trade-off between technician annotation time per participant (a key bottleneck) and PSG frequency—if mid-year data shows that technicians are drowning in annotation backlog, reactive adjustments to the schedule may be too late to prevent data quality degradation.

name: Semi-Automated Event-Triage Scope and Benchmark Framework
consequences: Aim 3 targets semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than autonomous diagnosis. The scope of triage (e.g., distinguishing parasomnia episodes from normal sleep movements, or from RBD-like behaviors) and the benchmark framework (sensitivity and specificity relative to dual human scoring, versus gold-standard polysomnographic criteria) shape both the algorithmic development effort and the publishability of the resulting model. A narrow scope (e.g., triage only flagged nights with clear morphologic evidence) is easier to train and validate but has limited clinical utility; a broad scope (e.g., distinguish all parasomnia types and rule out mimics) requires substantial algorithm development and larger training datasets but is more valuable for future clinical deployment. Dual human-scorer agreement as the benchmark is pragmatic but may enshrine inter-rater variability as a ceiling—if human scorers agree only 85% of the time, an algorithm achieving 87% agreement looks successful but may still misclassify 13% of events.
options: Narrow the triage scope to binary classification (flagged event versus artifact or normal movement) in year two, train the model on the first 30–40 adjudicated events, evaluate against dual human scoring agreement, publish methods and performance in a technical journal, and defer multi-class parasomnia-type discrimination to a follow-up phase-two study with a larger dataset.

Pursue multi-class triage (distinguish sleepwalking, confusional arousal, sleep terror, and RBD-like features) concurrently in year two using a hybrid human-in-the-loop framework: technicians annotate raw sensor signals with preliminary event labels in real time, a postdoc refines these with detailed review, and a machine-learning algorithm learns from the refined annotations, with sensitivity and specificity reported separately per parasomnia type.

Implement an external benchmark cohort: recruit 10–15 control participants and 5–10 additional NREM parasomnia participants outside the main admission pipeline, collect data with the same sensor suite but no clinical team blinding, share de-identified signals with a collaborating sleep neuroscience lab (blinded to diagnosis), and have that external team score events independently, using their agreement as the primary algorithm benchmark to reduce internal bias.
review: The core tension is algorithmic ambition versus evaluation honesty: multi-class triage is more ambitious and publishable, but human inter-rater agreement for parasomnia subtyping is often inconsistent, particularly for confusional arousals versus sleep terrors, which overlap clinically. None of the options address what happens if the technicians' real-time annotations in year one are poor quality (high disagreement with formal dual-scorer review), which would poison the training data for the algorithm—there is no built-in quality assurance loop to identify and retrain annotation practices before algorithm development begins.

name: Pharmaceutical Industry Partnership and Publication Independence
consequences: The plan describes a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement, with the academic team retaining full publication rights and pharma receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window. If a pharma partner is secured, it typically brings supplementary funding (e.g., €200–400K over 3 years for recruitment and data-sharing initiatives) and potential access to investigational sleep medications for exploratory mechanistic studies, but introduces reporting obligations, data-access timelines, and implicit incentives to generate results favorable to the sponsor's pipeline. Rejecting pharma funding preserves full editorial independence and reduces conflict-of-interest scrutiny, but increases reliance on DFG and internal University funding and may constrain the scope of exploratory mechanistic work (e.g., no pharmacologic intervention studies). The pre-publication comment window, while time-limited, can introduce delays and create tension if pharma identifies perceived misinterpretation of their compound's effects.
options: Commit at project start to funding from DFG and University of Bonn sources alone, explicitly decline pharma partnerships, and allocate the full €3.8M from public funding with no industry contingency, ensuring complete publication independence and simplifying ethics review while accepting that mechanistic studies using investigational compounds are out of scope.

Negotiate a pharma partnership at project start with explicit written terms: supplementary funding capped at €250K over 3 years, pre-publication review restricted to factual accuracy and IP disclosure (no editorial override), no preferential access to data during the funding period, and full publication independence for all Aim 1 and Aim 2 outputs; apply supplementary funds to enhanced sensor validation or control-cohort expansion.

Defer pharma partnership decision to month 12 (after initial recruitment and methods validation data are in hand), allowing the academic team to evaluate whether exploratory mechanistic studies (e.g., acute medication response profiling) are scientifically justified and whether the partnership terms are favorable; structure the contingent partnership to activate only if DFG or University funding lags recruitment targets.
review: The tension is scientific scope and independence versus operational funding security: pharma partnerships accelerate recruitment and mechanistic work but introduce reporting delays and potential conflicts-of-interest that are difficult to unwind mid-project. The options assume the academic team has sufficient grant-writing capacity and track record to secure competitive DFG funding without pharma co-support, which is uncertain at project launch—if DFG review is delayed or preliminary data are weak, pressures to accept pharma funding late in year one could force a rushed negotiation at a disadvantageous moment.

name: Success Metrics Threshold and Failure-Trigger Response Pathways
consequences: The plan specifies failure triggers at month 18: enrollment of fewer than 25 participants or capture of fewer than 40 adjudicated events triggers external review. These thresholds are absolute but not adaptive—if recruitment is slow due to referral-network delays or event yield is low due to tightened admission criteria or genuine low prevalence, a binary month-18 review may arrive too late to implement corrective action for year two or may recommend program termination even though the underlying issues are remediable. Conversely, setting thresholds too permissively (e.g., 20 participants and 30 events) delays corrective feedback and risks wasting budget on a fundamentally unviable model. The success criteria at 36 months (50–70 participants, 2–3 publications, benchmarked triage model) are broad; if achieved at any level (e.g., 50 participants at the lower bound, 1 publication instead of 3), the program is declared successful but may not have produced sufficient data for Aim 2 phenotyping or robust algorithm validation.
options: Lock the month-18 thresholds (25 participants, 40 events) and establish a pre-planned contingency protocol: if either threshold is missed, the external board convenes within 4 weeks, assesses whether the shortfall is due to recruitment strategy, admission criteria, or genuine low event yield, and mandates one of three responses—(i) modify recruitment channels or admission criteria by month 20, (ii) extend the program timeline by 6–12 months, or (iii) recommend termination with final data analysis and publication of feasibility findings.

Implement rolling monthly enrollment and event-yield tracking reported to the advisory board, with soft correction triggers at months 6, 12, and 18: if enrollment lags >30% of target, the board recommends recruitment-channel adjustments; if event yield is <1 event per 15 bed-nights, the board recommends admission-criteria review; only if both metrics remain subthreshold at month 18 does a formal failure review occur, allowing incremental corrective action without a sudden month-18 cliff.

Reframe the month-18 gate as a stage-gate decision rather than a binary success-or-failure: define preliminary success (15–20 participants, 25+ events) as sufficient to justify continued funding and proceed to enhanced-night PSG and algorithm development in year two; require full success (25+ participants, 40+ events) only at month 24 as a condition for year-three continuation; this distributes decision points across the funding cycle and allows mid-course learning.
review: The core tension is accountability versus adaptive management: binary failure triggers at fixed timepoints enforce discipline and deter indefinite extension, but real-world research programs often encounter unforeseen bottlenecks (e.g., slow ethics approval, unexpected turnover, seasonal recruitment variation) that are orthogonal to the quality of the scientific design. None of the options explicitly define what constitutes 'demonstrable progress' at month 24 for year-three contingency funding—whether progress means methodologically sound publications, preliminary phenotypic findings, or simply sustained operations, which leaves year-three continuation vulnerable to subjective judgment despite explicit thresholds for earlier gates.

name: Residential Layout Configuration and Behavioral Naturalism
consequences: Suite arrangement—whether clustered in pods with shared common areas or dispersed throughout the converted property—directly influences both the domestic feel that preserves parasomnia manifestation and the technician's ability to monitor safety and event response. A dispersed layout isolates participants but requires more infrastructure (additional alarm coverage, longer response times); pod clustering may amplify social inhibition and false reassurance that suppresses events.
options: Design the suites as semi-independent apartments scattered across three floors with private entrances and separate kitchenettes, forcing technicians to conduct area rounds every 15 minutes and rely on bedside contact sensors and audio for immediate event detection rather than visual presence.

Organize suites into two clusters of four around a central observation station with a shared common room and kitchenette, allowing technicians to remain in visual contact with most corridors and reducing response latency, at the cost of a more institutional feeling that may inhibit naturalistic parasomnia expression.

Implement a hybrid model with four dispersed suites in residential apartments across the neighborhood (but within a 3-minute walk of the main facility) and four in a small pod within the converted property, enabling recruitment flexibility, reduced on-site crowding, and testing whether remote sensing can reliably detect home-location events.
review: The core tension is that behavioral authenticity demands privacy and isolation, but safety and technician staffing ratios demand proximity and visual oversight. All three options assume that video or sensor coverage can substitute for technician presence during dispersed stays, yet the plan explicitly restricts video sharing and emphasizes physiological data; none of the options addresses whether contact-free mattress sensors and microphones alone are sufficient to detect seizure-like events, aspiration, or injuries in participants the technician cannot directly observe.

name: Pre-Admission Event Confirmation Pathway and Recruitment Speed
consequences: Requiring a baseline polysomnography or sleep study before enrollment ensures phenotypic homogeneity and captures one event at screening, reducing stay length and improving event-yield rates. Conversely, relying on clinical interview and collateral history accelerates recruitment, broadens the eligible pool, but increases the risk of zero-event admissions and lengthens residential stays to capture even one event, straining budget and staff.
options: Mandate that all applicants complete a single-night polysomnography at University Hospital Bonn or a regional sleep center before residential admission, with de-identified PSG reports reviewed by the PI to confirm event presence; participants demonstrating at least one recorded parasomnia event proceed directly to the residential unit, while those with negative PSG are deferred with the option to rescreen after 3 months.

Accept participants based solely on clinical interview, bed-partner collateral, self-report of at least 2 episodes per month, and PI adjudication without requiring prior polysomnography, thus accelerating enrollment and accepting that approximately 15–20% of admitted participants will produce zero events during their first 2-week residential stay and require extension or deferral.

Stratify recruitment into two parallel tracks: a 'confirmed' pathway requiring pre-admission PSG (priority enrollment, known event frequency) and a 'provisional' pathway for clinically typical patients without prior recording (lower priority, longer intended stays), allowing the facility to balance recruitment speed against event yield without rejecting any phenotypically appropriate referral.
review: The core tension is recruitment speed versus event-capture efficiency; longer pre-admission workup reduces residential stay length but delays enrollment and imposes costs on the referring system. All three options frame pre-screening as a recruitment filter, but none explicitly prices in the referral friction—neurologists and sleep clinics in Germany may not readily order additional PSG before residential referral, and patients face out-of-pocket cost and scheduling delay; the options do not account for how mandatory pre-PSG might reduce the referral pipeline by 30–50%.

name: Escalation-Monitoring Trigger Threshold and Algorithm Coupling
consequences: Setting when technicians deploy full PSG (e.g., only after visual or sensor confirmation of a probable event versus nightly on fixed nights regardless of activity) directly controls data volume, computational load, and event-triage algorithm development. Reactive escalation captures events with high resolution but misses subclinical arousals and creates data sparsity; routine escalation nights guarantee comparable physiological data but may introduce unnecessary PSG montage activation on quiet nights.
options: Deploy full PSG only on nights when the technician or low-burden sensors flag a probable parasomnia event (mattress movement spike, audio signature, EEG band-power anomaly), ensuring that enhanced recording is reactive and targeted; this minimizes PSG electrode fatigue and data volume but risks missing subtle or aborted events that occur between sensor check intervals.

Activate full PSG montage on a fixed 3-night schedule per participant (admission night, midstay, discharge) plus any flagged-event night, providing a consistent physiological baseline and spacing of comparative data while reducing technician discretion; this guarantees comparable reference data but may waste montage deployment on nights with zero events.

Implement a rolling 2-week adaptive window where full PSG deployment is randomized within each 2-week period and then repositioned based on real-time event density observed in the preceding week, ensuring that high-event-yield weeks receive more enhanced nights while low-yield weeks avoid unnecessary montage use, requiring algorithmic coordination with the technician team.
review: The core tension is between data comparability (fixed escalation) and resource efficiency (reactive escalation). The options assume technician event-flagging is reliable enough to guide PSG deployment, yet the plan acknowledges that Aim 3 is to develop semi-automated triage tools—implicitly recognizing that event detection is currently imperfect; using current technician judgment to gate enhanced monitoring creates a circularity where the detection benchmark is the very process being used to select which nights receive full data for algorithm training.

name: Matched Control Recruitment Strategy and Facility Throughput
consequences: Enrolling matched controls (healthy participants with no parasomnia history) simultaneously with patients enables direct comparison of sensor false-positive rates and baseline arousal patterns, but requires recruiting an additional cohort, training them identically, and using residential suites on nights when they could house symptomatic participants. Deferring controls to a later 'phase 2' or using retrospective normative data from public PSG databases reduces operational overhead but sacrifices specificity and requires statistical adjustment.
options: Enroll matched controls concurrently with patients from the outset, targeting a 1:1 ratio (if 60 patients enrolled, admit 60 controls), giving each control a 3–4 night residential stay under identical monitoring conditions; this provides direct sensor baseline and maximizes statistical power for triage-tool benchmarking but locks up approximately 20% of residential suite capacity for non-diagnostic participants throughout the 3-year period.

Defer matched control enrollment until year 2 after Aim 1 operational validation is complete, then conduct a focused 6-month control cohort of 20–25 healthy participants in parallel with ongoing patient recruitment; this preserves year-1 throughput for symptomatic participants and reduces year-1 budget but defers the benchmarking dataset and requires retrospective statistical adjustment for algorithm development.

Recruit and admit controls only during months when patient referral is slow or facility occupancy dips below 60%, treating control enrollment as a flexible 'buffer' activity that fills available capacity without displacing patient admissions; controls are prioritized for weeks with fewer pending diagnostic admissions, ensuring no diagnostic participant waits for a suite.
review: The core tension is between scientific rigor (concurrent controls with full comparability) and operational efficiency (deferred or opportunistic controls). All three options assume that healthy participants will consent to 3–4 nights of overnight monitoring in an unfamiliar facility, yet the plan does not disclose the expected control recruitment rate—they may be substantially harder to enroll than patients motivated by clinical diagnosis; the options also ignore whether controls' presence in a psychiatric- or epilepsy-adjacent facility might bias their behavior or arousal patterns relative to true home environment data.

name: Inter-Rater Scoring Burden and Centralized Annotation Model
consequences: Requiring two independent human raters to score every parasomnia event (per Aim 3 benchmark) ensures reliable ground truth but generates high labor costs for post-hoc scoring; having the night technician perform real-time annotation and one secondary rater review offline reduces labor but couples operational staffing to scientific scoring and risks bias if the same technician repeatedly scores their own flags. Outsourcing scoring to an external clinical center introduces delay and cost but decouples operational from scientific judgment.
options: Employ the night technician as the first annotator during real-time monitoring and immediately after each shift, then contract two independent sleep medicine specialists (postdoctoral researchers or regional board-certified clinicians) to independently re-score all flagged events offline using AASM criteria, creating a gold-standard dual-rater consensus with techncian annotation as reference; this ensures independent verification but costs approximately €400–600 per week in external scoring fees.

Assign real-time annotation to the night technician and designate one of the two postdoctoral researchers to conduct independent offline review of all flagged events within one week of capture; this reduces external costs but couples scientific scoring to the academic research staff, creating potential scheduling conflicts and fatigue during high-event-yield weeks.

Establish a regional consortium with one partner sleep clinic (e.g., University of Düsseldorf or Charité Berlin) to provide independent dual-rater scoring of approximately 50% of flagged events (randomly selected) and compare their adjudication against the Bonn team's dual-rater consensus; use the comparison to estimate inter-center reliability and apply it to the full dataset, reducing overall scoring burden while quantifying site-level bias.
review: The core tension is scoring labor cost versus independence of judgment. Outsourcing dual scoring to external clinicians ensures independence but delays dataset closure (external annotators may have 6–8 week turnaround), making iterative algorithm refinement slower; using the postdoctoral researchers as independent raters couples academic publication authorship to scoring role, creating potential conflicts when those researchers co-author methods papers based on their own annotations. None of the options address training and inter-rater reliability between the technician and external raters at project outset—rater drift over 3 years could substantially alter consensus criteria.

name: Data Sharing Timeline and Repository Selection Strategy
consequences: Depositing de-identified physiological data early in project Year 2 accelerates community access and citation potential, enables external algorithm validation, and attracts collaborators, but may preempt the Bonn team's planned publications or seed competition from better-resourced labs. Deferring deposit until after primary papers are published (Year 3) protects novelty but delays open science impact and may reduce data re-use if the field has moved on.
options: Release de-identified physiological data and sensor streams to OpenNeuro.org and the German Data Forum (German Genetic Variation Database or similar national resource) by month 18, contingent on at least 50 enrolled participants and 40 adjudicated events captured; this maximizes open science visibility and enables rapid external validation but risks competing publications and requires robust de-identification and data dictionary documentation.

Embargo data until 18 months after the last participant is discharged (approximately month 26 in the 3-year timeline), during which the Bonn team publishes Aims 1–3 results; deposit to a general sleep physiology repository in Year 3; this protects publication priority but delays community access and may reduce secondary-use opportunities if external groups have already developed competing algorithms.

Establish a data-sharing agreement requiring external researchers to apply for access through a lightweight request process and pledge to cite the Bonn team's methods paper and share results of any reanalysis before independent publication, enabling controlled release by month 20 with publication safeguards; data are initially hosted on a university repository rather than a public archive.
review: The core tension is between open science principles (early release) and competitive protection (delayed release). The plan specifies that public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data without video, and that a dataset will be deposited in a 'recognized repository'—but none of the options address whether OpenNeuro (which is BIDS-compliant but often requires very liberal reuse terms) aligns with the pharmaceutical partnership's interests if a pharma deal includes data-use restrictions; early release could inadvertently bind a future industry partner to open-science commitments the Bonn team has not yet negotiated.

name: External Expertise Intake Model and Advisory Independence
consequences: A 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months provides institutional distance and protective credibility but may become detached from operational realities and offer generic feedback if meetings are virtual or board members lack hands-on facility familiarity. Embedding a clinical advisor on-site part-time ensures real-time guidance on participant safety and event interpretation but risks editorial conflicts and may reduce board independence if that advisor becomes invested in project success.
options: Maintain the planned 3-member external SAB with in-person semi-annual site visits (2–3 days each in Bonn), including direct observation of the facility, review of real-time data, and discussion with the core team; board members are selected for expertise in parasomnia neurology, pediatric parasomnias or neurodevelopmental phenotyping (for conceptual cross-pollination), and sleep physiology infrastructure; meetings are documented and minutes are filed with the funding agency.

Appoint a part-time embedded clinical advisor (0.2 FTE) who is a board-certified sleep specialist from another German medical center (e.g., Charité or Frankfurt), physically present on-site one morning per week during the first 18 months, available for consultation on participant safety, unusual events, and clinical judgment calls, with quarterly remote meetings with the external SAB; this creates real-time clinical escalation capacity but risks the advisor becoming operationally entangled.

Split advisory roles into a scientific advisory board (3 members, remote semi-annual meetings focused on publication strategy and Aim 2–3 benchmarking) and a separate safety and operations committee (2 members from German sleep medicine and anesthesia, in-person quarterly site visits focused on facility safety, staffing, and participant welfare), with formal separation of remits and documented escalation procedures between committees.
review: The core tension is between advisory independence and actionable on-site clinical judgment. An external SAB meeting twice yearly by videoconference may miss operational crises (e.g., a participant with subtle seizure-like activity, staffing burnout, repeated false alarms), while an embedded clinical advisor reduces advisory independence and may pressure the PI toward conservative interventions that constrain event capture. None of the options explicitly address whether the advisor or SAB has the authority to recommend halting enrollment or facility closure if safety concerns emerge, or whether that authority remains exclusively with the PI and the ethics committee.

name: Pharmaceutical Industry Partnership Entry Point and Scope Boundary
consequences: Partnering with a pharmaceutical company early (Year 1 pre-recruitment) provides guaranteed funding upside and in-kind research resources (e.g., biomarker assays, secondary outcome measures), aligns the facility with industry clinical development timelines, but risks biasing recruitment toward phenotypes that match a pharma sponsor's drug target or creating perception of bias in event-triage algorithm development. Remaining independent until Year 2 and negotiating partnership only if needed protects scientific autonomy but leaves funding uncertain if the DFG grant faces revision.
options: Pursue a pharma partnership by month 6 targeting a neuropsychiatric or sleep-focused biopharmaceutical company with interests in parasomnia neurobiology; scope partnership to secondary biomarker collection (blood/CSF sampling on subset of nights) and post-hoc mechanistic analysis explicitly decoupled from Aims 1–3 primary outcomes; document the 30-day pre-publication review window contractually and reserve right to publish primary results without pharma consent.

Remain operationally independent throughout the 3-year study period, relying exclusively on DFG and University of Bonn funding; invite potential pharma partners to observe the final 6-month data review and results validation, positioning any partnership as a Phase 2 opportunity only after primary publications are complete; this preserves autonomy but risks funding shortfalls if the DFG grant requires cost-sharing beyond university capacity.

Establish a loosely coupled funding consortium where a pharma partner provides capital (estimated 15–20% of Year 2–3 budget, approximately €200–250K) without data ownership or pre-publication rights, with the understanding that the partner may license results after 2 years post-publication for their own drug development; the Bonn team retains all publication and IP rights, and the partner is invited to joint authorship only if they directly contribute scientific labor.
review: The core tension is between funding certainty and scientific independence. The plan states that industry partnership is 'optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement,' yet all three options either accelerate partnership (introducing potential bias) or defer it (risking budget shortfalls). The options do not clarify whether a pharma partner would fund only secondary biomarkers or would co-design inclusion/exclusion criteria to match a clinical phenotype of interest—if the latter, recruitment criteria could shift away from the current AASM-based definition of NREM parasomnia toward a narrower industry-relevant phenotype, de facto rescoping Aim 2 without explicit acknowledgment.
